content.text: Humans and all living organisms form a complex web of ecological system in which we are only a part and very much dependent on this system for our own existence. For example, plants, animals, and micro-organisms re-create the quality of air we breathe, the water we drink, and the soil that produces our food.

content.title: Role of Forests
content.text: Forests play a key role in the ecological system as they are the primary producers on which all other living beings depend.

Biodiversity or Biological Diversity is immensely rich in wildlife and cultivated species, diverse in form and function but closely integrated in a system through multiple networks of interdependencies.

India is one of the world’s richest countries in terms of its vast array of biological diversity. This richness is so well integrated in our daily life that we often take it for granted. However, these resources are under great stress due to environmental insensitivity.

notes.section_title: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

content.title: Why Conserve?
items: Preserves ecological diversity and life support systems (water, air, soil).
Preserves genetic diversity of plants and animals for better growth and breeding.
Ensures survival of traditional crop varieties and maintenance of aquatic biodiversity for fisheries.

content.text: Implemented in 1972, this act provided various provisions for protecting habitats. It included an all-India list of protected species and focused on protecting endangered species by banning hunting, giving legal protection to habitats, and restricting trade in wildlife.

content.title: Specific Animal Projects
content.text: The central government announced projects for protecting specific threatened animals, including the tiger, one-horned rhinoceros, Kashmir stag (hangul), three types of crocodiles, and the Asiatic lion.

Launched in 1973, Project Tiger is one of the most well-publicised wildlife campaigns. It was initiated after realizing that the tiger population had dwindled from 55,000 at the turn of the century to just 1,827 in 1973.

content.title: Major Threats to Tigers
items: Poaching for trade (skins and bones for traditional medicines).
Shrinking habitat due to human population growth.

notes.section_title: Types and Distribution of Forest Resources

rows: Regarded as most valuable for conservation of forest and wildlife. Over half of total forest land.
Large percentages in J&K, Andhra, Kerala, TN, West Bengal, Maharashtra.
Protected from any further depletion. Almost one-third of total forest area.
Bulk of area in Bihar, Haryana, Punjab, Himachal, Odisha, Rajasthan.
Wastelands belonging to both government and private individuals/communities.
High percentage in North-eastern states and parts of Gujarat.

content.title: Permanent Forest Estates
content.text: Reserved and protected forests are referred to as permanent forest estates, maintained for timber production and protective reasons. Madhya Pradesh has the largest area (75%) under permanent forests.
In many parts of India, local communities are struggling to conserve habitats along with government officials, recognizing the link to their own long-term livelihood.

content.title: Community Initiatives
items: Sariska Tiger Reserve: Villagers fought against mining citing the Wildlife Protection Act.
Alwar District (Rajasthan): Inhabitants of five villages declared 1,200 hectares as 'Bhairodev Dakav Sonchuri' with their own rules.
Chipko Movement: Resisted deforestation in the Himalayas and promoted community afforestation.
Beej Bachao Andolan (Tehri): Revived traditional farming without synthetic chemicals.

content.text: Based on the premis that all creations of nature have to be protected, sacred groves (forests of God and Goddesses) are patches of forest left untouched by local people. Interference with them is banned.

content.title: Tree Worship Examples
items: Mundas and Santhals: Worship mahua and kadamba trees.
Tribals of Odisha and Bihar: Worship tamarind and mango trees during weddings.
Pan-India: Peepal and banyan trees are considered sacred.

content.text: The JFM programme involves local communities in the management and restoration of degraded forests. It has been in formal existence since 1988 (first resolution passed in Odisha).

content.title: Benefits of JFM
content.text: Communities protect degraded forest land managed by the Forest Department. In return, they get intermediary benefits like non-timber forest produce and a share in the timber harvested by successful protection.

glossary.definition: The variety of all living organisms, including genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity, integrated through a network of interdependencies.
A complex web of living and non-living components where organisms depend on each other and their environment for survival.
Forest land declared by the government as most valuable for the conservation of timber and wildlife resources.
Forest areas protected by the government from any further depletion or encroachment.
Forests and wastelands belonging to both government and private individuals or communities, often managed locally.
Patches of virgin forest preserved in their pristine form due to religious beliefs and nature worship.
A programme involving the collaboration between the state forest department and local communities to manage and restore degraded forests.

summary_points: Forests are the primary producers and a key component of the complex ecological web upon which human survival depends.
India possesses immense biological diversity which is currently under stress due to industrial and human insensitivity.
The Indian Wildlife (Protection) Act of 1972 and Project Tiger (1973) represent landmark institutional steps toward conserving endangered species.
Forests are classified into Reserved, Protected, and Unclassed categories, with Reserved forests being the most valuable for conservation.
Local communities have historically played a vital role in conservation through movements like Chipko, JFM, and the protection of Sacred Groves.
Sustainable resource management in India relies on the active involvement of communities and the integration of traditional beliefs with modern conservation strategies.
